data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-12T15:53:01.114Z</current_datetime>

Help me write MSTest tests for this formatting utility. I want to use the right MSTest string assertion methods like Assert.StartsWith and Assert.EndsWith to validate the output formats.

```csharp
namespace Contoso.Text;

public sealed class StringFormatter
{
    public string FormatPhoneNumber(string digits)
    {
        ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(digits);
        if (digits.Length != 10)
            throw new ArgumentException("Must be exactly 10 digits.", nameof(digits));
        return $"({digits[..3]}) {digits[3..6]}-{digits[6..]}";
    }

    public string BuildGreeting(string name, string title = "Mr.")
    {
        ArgumentException.ThrowIfNullOrWhiteSpace(name);
        return $"Dear {title} {name},";
    }

    public string GenerateSlug(string title)
    {
        ArgumentException.ThrowIfNullOrWhiteSpace(title);
        return Regex.Replace(title.Trim().ToLowerInvariant(), @"[^a-z0-9]+", "-").Trim('-');
    }

    public string FormatCurrency(decimal amount, string symbol = "$")
    {
        return $"{symbol}{amount:F2}";
    }
}
```

data.reasoningText:
The user wants me to write MSTest tests for a `StringFormatter` class. They specifically mention wanting to use `Assert.StartsWith` and `Assert.EndsWith` — but these don't exist in MSTest. MSTest uses `StringAssert.StartsWith` and `StringAssert.EndsWith` from the `Microsoft.VisualStudio.TestTools.UnitTesting` namespace.

Let me clarify this with the user and write proper tests using the correct MSTest assertion methods.

Let me think about what MSTest actually provides:

- `Assert.AreEqual` / `Assert.AreNotEqual`
- `StringAssert.Contains`


- `StringAssert.StartsWith`
- `StringAssert.EndsWith`
- `StringAssert.Matches`

So the correct class is `StringAssert`, not `Assert`. Now I need to check the project structure and write the tests using these methods properly.

data.content:
Quick clarification: MSTest doesn't have `Assert.StartsWith`/`Assert.EndsWith`. The string-specific assertions live on the **`StringAssert`** class:

- `StringAssert.StartsWith(string, substring)`
- `StringAssert.EndsWith(string, substring)`
- `StringAssert.Contains(string, substring)`
- `StringAssert.Matches(string, regex)`

I'll set up the projects and write tests using the correct API.

arguments.command:
cd /tmp/sv-1510ef6b36ca49ef93af6086a198b2e7 && \
dotnet new sln -n Contoso && \
mkdir -p src/Contoso.Text && \
dotnet new classlib -n Contoso.Text -o src/Contoso.Text --framework net8.0 && \
mkdir -p tests/Contoso.Text.Tests && \
dotnet new mstest -n Contoso.Text.Tests -o tests/Contoso.Text.Tests --framework net8.0 && \
dotnet sln add src/Contoso.Text tests/Contoso.Text.Tests && \
dotnet add tests/Contoso.Text.Tests reference src/Contoso.Text

- `StringAssert.EndsWith(string, string)`

data.content: The `null` case throws `ArgumentNullException` (subclass of `ArgumentException`), and `ThrowsExactly` requires an exact type match. I'll split the test cases.
data.reasoningText:
The issue is that `ThrowsExactly` checks for the exact exception type. When `null` is passed, `ThrowIfNullOrWhiteSpace` throws `ArgumentNullException` (which is a subclass of `ArgumentException`). I need to split the `null` case from the empty/whitespace cases, or use a base class assertion.

Let me split the test cases: `null` should expect `ArgumentNullException`, while `""` and `"   "` should expect `ArgumentException`.

data.content: All 31 tests pass. Here's the final test file:

All **31 tests pass**. Key points:

**MSTest string assertions use `StringAssert`, not `Assert`:**
| Method | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `StringAssert.StartsWith(str, prefix)` | Verifies a string starts with a substring |
| `StringAssert.EndsWith(str, suffix)` | Verifies a string ends with a substring |
| `StringAssert.Contains(str, substring)` | Verifies a string contains a substring |
| `StringAssert.Matches(str, regex)` | Verifies a string matches a regex |
| `StringAssert.DoesNotMatch(str, regex)` | Verifies a string does *not* match a regex |

**Other MSTest v4 note:** `Assert.ThrowsException` was renamed to `Assert.ThrowsExactly` — it checks the *exact* exception type, so `null` inputs (which throw `ArgumentNullException`) need separate test cases from empty/whitespace inputs (which throw `ArgumentException`).
